metaDescription: Free CARSI course on drying flood-affected transport assets — containment, air-quality monitoring, drying metals, plastics and composites, and compliance.
imageAlt: Restoration technician drying a flood-damaged vehicle interior with air movers in an Australian workshop setting.

og.title: Drying Transportation & Vehicles — Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free introduction to drying flood-affected transport assets: containing contaminants, monitoring air quality, and drying moisture-sensitive metals, plastics, insulations and composites.

faq.q: What does the Introduction to Drying Transportation and Vehicles course cover?
faq.a: This free CARSI course introduces how to dry and restore flood-affected assets — protecting equipment and stock, containing contaminants, monitoring indoor air quality, drying moisture-sensitive materials, and meeting regulatory compliance requirements.

faq.q: How are moisture-sensitive materials dried after flooding?
faq.a: The course covers material-specific drying: desiccant pouches to draw subsurface humidity from dense metals such as aluminium, replacing waterlogged porous insulations to prevent microbial growth, infrared thermal mapping to dry textured plastics evenly, and negative-pressure cycling to remove moisture from composite materials.

faq.q: Why is air-quality monitoring important during flood recovery?
faq.a: Monitoring detects volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and other airborne contaminants early, giving time to adjust ventilation and prevent contamination spreading. The course also covers multistage indoor air-quality testing to confirm a site is safe before operations resume.
No. This is a free introductory online course with no prerequisites — suited to Australian restoration professionals and anyone starting out in flood-damage recovery.
